questions.question: What does acting with dignity mean when giving personal care?
options: Treating the person as a valued human being and protecting their self-respect, whatever help they need
Only helping people who can no longer speak
Making all the person's decisions for them
Getting the task done as quickly as you can

questions.question: Which set of actions best protects a person's privacy during personal care?
options: Leaving the door open so colleagues can see you are working
Talking loudly so everyone knows what care is being given
Uncovering the whole body at once to save time
Closing doors and curtains, covering the parts of the body you are not washing, and knocking and waiting before entering

questions.question: During personal care you learn something private about a person. How should you treat this information?
options: Keep it confidential and share only what colleagues need to know to give safe care
Share it freely with anyone who asks
Discuss it openly in front of the person
Write it on a public noticeboard

questions.question: What does it mean to gain a person's consent for personal care?
options: The person understands what you are offering and freely agrees to it
The task is written in the care plan, so no agreement is needed
You have decided the care is good for them
A relative has agreed on the person's behalf without asking them

questions.question: Part way through helping someone shower, they ask to stop. What should you do?
options: Stop, respect their decision, check they are safe and comfortable, and report the refusal so it can be recorded
Carry on because the task is nearly finished
Leave them alone in the shower without telling anyone
Tell them they must continue as it is in their care plan

questions.question: Which is good communication practice when giving personal care?
options: Talk about the person to a colleague as though they were not there
Touch the person first and explain afterwards
Tell the person what you are going to do before you do it, and check it is alright
Rush the person so they do not have time to respond

questions.question: You are unsure about a person's cultural or religious wishes around washing. What is the best approach?
options: Ignore culture because it is not part of personal care
Ask, listen and respect, checking their care plan and asking the person or family kindly rather than assuming
Assume their wishes based on their appearance
Decide it does not matter and carry on as usual

questions.question: Before helping someone to wash, how should you prepare the environment?
options: Only prepare the room if the person asks you to
Leave the person undressed while you look for a towel
Start straight away and fetch things as you need them
Gather towels, soap, clean clothes and equipment first, and make the room private and warm

questions.question: Why must you test the water temperature before a person gets into a bath or shower?
options: To make the wash quicker
Only because the person expects it
Because water that is too hot can scald the person
There is no need to test it if the room is warm

questions.question: When should you carry out hand hygiene during personal care?
options: Only if the person appears to be unwell
Before and after giving personal care, after using the toilet, and whenever your hands are visibly dirty
Only after you have finished all your visits for the day
Only at the start of your shift

questions.question: What is the correct approach to single-use gloves and aprons?
options: Wear the same PPE for several people to save supplies
Only wear PPE when a person looks unwell
Reuse gloves if they still look clean
Put on fresh PPE for each task and each person, and dispose of it in the correct waste

questions.question: How should you promote a person's independence during personal care?
options: Rush them so they do not get tired
Do everything for them so it is done properly
Offer help but let the person try first, use aids and gentle prompts, and give time and praise
Only let them help if it saves you time

questions.question: A person finds open questions hard to answer. How can you still offer them a real choice of clothing?
options: Choose for them without asking
Insist they answer an open question
Tell them their choice does not matter
Hold up two tops so they can respond to the option they can see

questions.question: In which direction should you wash a person's body?
options: From the least clean area to the cleanest area
Always start with the genital area
It does not matter which way you wash
From the cleanest area to the least clean area

questions.question: Why is it important to dry carefully between skin folds and toes?
options: It makes the wash quicker to finish
Moisture left on the skin can lead to soreness, breakdown and infection
Drying is not necessary if the room is warm
It is only important for people who can walk

questions.question: How often should you help a person with brushing their teeth or cleaning their dentures?
options: Only on bath days
Only when they complain of pain
Once a week
Twice a day

questions.question: According to the course, whose nails should only be cut by a specialist?
options: Anyone who asks for help
A person with diabetes, as their care plan may say only a specialist should cut their nails
No one, care workers may always cut nails
Only people who can walk

questions.question: When helping someone with weakness on one side to dress, which side do you put clothing onto first?
options: Whichever side is nearest to you
It makes no difference which side
The weaker or more painful side first
The stronger side first

questions.question: What is good practice when supporting someone with toileting or continence?
options: Respond promptly, give as much privacy as is safe, keep skin clean and dry, and wear appropriate PPE
Make them wait so you can finish other tasks
Leave the door wide open at all times
Show that it is an inconvenience so they ask less often

questions.question: While washing someone, you see redness over the base of the spine that does not fade when pressed. What should you do?
options: Avoid massaging the area and report it promptly so it can be assessed and acted on
Wait a few days to see whether it gets worse before telling anyone
Rub the area firmly to improve the blood flow
Ignore it, as redness is always harmless

questions.question: Which bony areas should you look at closely for early signs of pressure damage?
options: Heels, hips, base of the spine and elbows
Only the stomach
Only the face
Only the palms of the hands

questions.question: You notice a change in a person while giving personal care but you are not sure what is causing it. What is your role?
options: To notice the change and pass it on, without needing to give a diagnosis
To diagnose the cause yourself before telling anyone
To decide it is not important and say nothing
To wait until you are certain what it is

questions.question: A usually cheerful person has become quiet and withdrawn over several visits. What should you do?
options: Decide it must be nothing and forget about it
Say nothing, as mood is not part of personal care
Report the change so the team can look into it, even though you cannot say what is causing it
Wait until the next monthly review to mention it

questions.question: A person refuses to be washed. What is the right response?
options: Shame them into agreeing
Stay calm, offer gently, try again later, explore the reason, and report worrying or repeated refusals
Force the task quickly before they can object
Insist and wash them anyway because it is good for them

questions.question: While helping someone dress, you notice unexplained bruising and signs the person is frightened. What must you do?
options: Keep it to yourself to avoid trouble
Ask the person's neighbour what they think
Wait to see if it happens again
Report the safeguarding concern straight away, following your employer's policy

questions.question: You should test the temperature of the water before a person gets into a bath or shower.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is acceptable to continue personal care after a person withdraws consent, as long as the task is nearly finished.
options: True
False

questions.question: Hand hygiene is the single most important way to prevent the spread of infection.
options: True
False

questions.question: Single-use gloves may be reused for the next task if they still look clean.
options: True
False

questions.question: When dressing a person with weakness on one side, you should put clothing onto the weaker side first.
options: True
False

questions.question: If you see redness that does not fade when pressed, you should massage the area firmly to improve blood flow.
options: True
False

questions.question: A person's cultural and religious wishes should be recorded in their care plan and followed as carefully as any other part of their care.
options: True
False

questions.question: Reporting what you notice by word of mouth is enough, and there is no need to write it down.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are core values that run through good personal care? (Select all that apply)
options: Privacy
Taking over every task for the person
Respect
Dignity
Working as fast as possible

questions.question: Which actions help protect a person's privacy during personal care? (Select all that apply)
options: Discussing the person's difficulties in front of them
Leaving the door open so others can watch
Knocking and waiting before entering a room
Covering the parts of the body you are not washing
Closing doors and curtains before anyone undresses

questions.question: Which of these can be early signs of pressure damage that you should report? (Select all that apply)
options: Skin that is a normal colour and comfortable
Redness that does not fade when pressed
An area that is unusually warm
Skin that is hard or painful
Clothing the person has chosen themselves

questions.question: Which of the following are part of good infection prevention and control during personal care? (Select all that apply)
options: Reusing single-use gloves between people
Skipping hand hygiene when you are busy
Wearing gloves and an apron for tasks where you may touch bodily fluids
Handling soiled laundry and clinical waste according to your employer's policy
Washing your hands before and after giving personal care

questions.question: Which changes in a person are worth noticing and reporting to the care team? (Select all that apply)
options: Low mood, confusion, or new difficulty with tasks
Weight loss or gain, poor appetite, or new pain
The person choosing a different top to wear today
Redness, broken skin, rashes, bruising or swelling
The person preferring a shower to a bath

questions.question: What makes a good care record? (Select all that apply)
options: Clear and factual, avoiding opinion
Left for several days before writing it up
Accurate, writing what you actually saw or did
Timely, recorded as soon as you can while it is fresh
Based mainly on your assumptions

questions.question: Which actions help you promote a person's independence during personal care? (Select all that apply)
options: Doing everything for them to save time
Rushing the person so care is finished quickly
Letting the person try before you step in
Giving time and praise rather than rushing
Using aids and gentle prompts so tasks stay within reach

questions.question: Always gain a person's ___ before you begin personal care.
___ is described as the single most important way to prevent the spread of infection.
When washing a person, always work from the ___ area to the least clean area.
Redness that does not ___ when pressed can be an early sign of a pressure ulcer.
When helping someone with weakness on one side to dress, put clothing onto the ___ side first.
